title: pgvector for Vector PM — strategic vector search across the platform
topic: pgvector + self-hosted embeddings as a cross-cutting capability for Vector PM (search, dedup, linking, RAG, docs)
summary: Validates R010's pgvector + Ollama + NOTIFY/LISTEN bet; flags HNSW over IVFFlat, durable queue, and pre-filtering as the three corrections.
content:
0. Executive verdict

R010 commits to four foundational decisions: pgvector over standalone vector DBs, self-hosted Ollama for embeddings, hybrid TSVECTOR+vector search via Reciprocal Rank Fusion, and async indexing via Postgres NOTIFY/LISTEN. After fetching the primary sources (pgvector GitHub README v0.8.2, Supabase hybrid-search docs, Ollama model pages, MTEB scores, AWS pgvector benchmarks, the November-2025 "case against pgvector" essay, and 2025-2026 production stories), the verdict is: three of the four decisions hold up cleanly; one needs amending.

  • pgvector — correct for Vector PM's scale (single-tenant subscriptions, expected sub-10M vectors per tenant, Postgres-native joins to visibility filters). The case-against essay is real but applies to corpora >50M vectors with hot reindexing — not Vector PM in Phase 1.
  • Ollama + open-source embedding models — correct, with a specific recommendation: nomic-embed-text-v1.5 (768-dim, Apache 2.0, Matryoshka-truncatable to 256/512, 8K context) is the better default than the originally listed candidates. bge-base-en-v1.5 is the runner-up. all-MiniLM-L6-v2 is the budget fallback.
  • Hybrid search via RRF — correct, with concrete SQL recipes from Supabase and pgvector docs. R010's example in section 14.6 uses weighted score fusion; switch to true RRF (1.0/(rrf_k+rank)) — it's the documented Supabase pattern and is more robust to score-scale skew.
  • NOTIFY/LISTEN queue — partially correct. NOTIFY/LISTEN alone is at-most-once and silently drops messages on connection loss; the 8GB queue can deadlock if any listener holds a long transaction. Amend to: outbox table + LISTEN/NOTIFY as a wake-up signal + secondary polling for missed rows. This is the standard pattern (Procrastinate, River, several Postgres-native job queues) and turns the worker into at-least-once delivery without adding infrastructure.

R010's section 14.5 NOTIFY/LISTEN trigger as written will lose embeddings under load or restart. The fix is small (add a queue table, mark rows processed, poll for unprocessed) but it must land in Phase 1, not be deferred.

A. pgvector itself (the foundation)

A.1 What it is, who maintains it, current version, license

pgvector is an open-source Postgres extension for vector similarity search, maintained by Andrew Kane and contributors. Current version is 0.8.2 as of the source-of-truth GitHub README. Released under a permissive open-source licence (PostgreSQL licence — BSD-equivalent). It supports PostgreSQL 13 and later. Installation is available via Docker, Homebrew, PGXN, APT, Yum, pkg, APK, conda-forge, Postgres.app, or manual make install. No subscription, no paid tier, no telemetry — fully aligned with the Vector PM open-source-first principle.

A.2 How it integrates with Postgres

pgvector is a Postgres extension: CREATE EXTENSION vector; registers a new vector(N) type, three distance operators (<-> L2, <#> negative inner product, <=> cosine), three more for L1/Hamming/Jaccard, and two index access methods (IVFFlat and HNSW). Storage is in the table's heap exactly like any other column — no shadow store. Vectors participate in transactions, replicas, backups, point-in-time recovery, RLS, and JOINs the same way every other column does. This is the structural advantage over standalone vector DBs.

A.3 Index types — IVFFlat vs HNSW

Two ANN (approximate nearest neighbour) indexes are available. The decision matrix from the AWS pgvector benchmark (58,600 documents at 1,536 dimensions):

MethodQuery latencyBuild timeMemoryRecallUpdate cost
Sequential scan~650 ms0low1.0 (perfect)none
IVFFlat~2.4 ms~15 slow~0.95 with tuningcentroids drift, periodic REINDEX
HNSW~1.5 ms~30–81 s2–3× the data size~0.99 with defaultsgraph maintains itself, no REINDEX

HNSW parameters: m (max connections per layer, default 16), ef_construction (candidate list during build, default 64), ef_search (candidate list at query time, default 40). Higher = better recall, slower.

IVFFlat parameters: lists (= rows/1000 below 1M rows; sqrt(rows) above 1M), probes (default 1, raise for recall).

Critical IVFFlat constraint: the index needs data to compute centroids — you must load rows first, then CREATE INDEX. With HNSW you can create the empty index on an empty table and inserts populate the graph. For Vector PM's pattern (continuous writes, no bulk-load step), HNSW is the correct default.

R010 amendment: R010 section 14.2 specifies USING ivfflat. Change to USING hnsw. Reasons: (1) Vector PM writes continuously and never bulk-loads, so IVFFlat's load-first constraint is awkward; (2) HNSW gives ~99% recall with default parameters, IVFFlat needs tuning per-table; (3) HNSW build is now ~30× faster than pgvector 0.5.1 (parallel builds in 0.6.2, more in 0.7.0); (4) HNSW handles the Vector PM scale (probably <10M vectors per subscription, given the artefact-counts implied) on a single Postgres instance with comfortable headroom.

A.4 Distance metrics

OperatorMetricWhen to useIndex op-class
<=>Cosine distanceDefault for text embeddings — measures angle, not magnitude. nomic-embed-text and BGE both expect this.vector_cosine_ops
<#>Negative inner productFaster than cosine if vectors are normalised (most modern embedding models output unit vectors); equivalent ranking.vector_ip_ops
<->L2 (Euclidean)Image embeddings, raw feature vectors that aren't unit-normalised.vector_l2_ops
<+>L1 (taxicab)Specialist; rarely used for text.vector_l1_ops
Hamming / JaccardBinary vectorsUse after binary quantisation for ultra-cheap storage at scale.bit_hamming_ops / bit_jaccard_ops

Vector PM recommendation: use vector_cosine_ops by default. Both nomic-embed-text and BGE return unit vectors, so vector_ip_ops would also work and is microscopically faster — but cosine is the documented convention for semantic search and trades zero ranking quality.

A.5 Vector dimensions and scale characteristics

Limits per the README:

  • Standard vector: up to 16,000 dimensions (4 bytes per element)
  • halfvec: up to 16,000 dimensions at 2 bytes per element — quantises to fp16 with negligible recall loss
  • bit: up to 64,000 dimensions for binary vectors
  • sparsevec: up to 16,000 non-zero elements

Practical scale signal points:

  • Below ~100k vectors: index optional, sequential scan is <50 ms.
  • 100k–1M: HNSW essential; defaults work; comfortable single-instance.
  • 1M–10M: HNSW with m=16 default still works; consider halfvec to halve RAM. Discourse runs pgvector on this band across thousands of databases.
  • 10M–100M: tuning territory — quantisation (halfvec or binary), partitioning by tenant, possibly partial indexes per tenant. One reference e-commerce deployment hit 50M vectors / 300 GB / ~1 TB RAM for full in-memory HNSW.
  • >100M: where the case-against-pgvector argument starts to bite. Qdrant's memory-mapped disk-backed HNSW is 3–5× more memory-efficient at this scale. Vector PM is unlikely to be here for years; this is a Phase 4+ concern.

A.6 Maintenance characteristics

  • HNSW: graph self-maintains on insert/update/delete. No periodic REINDEX. Soft-deleted rows stay in the graph until VACUUM removes them — long-soft-delete tables benefit from periodic VACUUM.
  • IVFFlat: centroids are computed once at CREATE INDEX time. As data shifts, centroids become stale and recall degrades. Periodic REINDEX is the documented mitigation.
  • Write amplification: HNSW updates are O(log n) per row — graph is touched on every insert. With async embedding (NOTIFY/LISTEN worker), this happens off the user's write path so the latency cost is hidden.
  • Partial indexes (WHERE archived_at IS NULL) keep indexes lean — strongly recommended; R010 14.2 already does this.

A.7 Production stories (dated)

  • Discourse (2025): pgvector across thousands of databases, billions of pageviews. Uses halfvec for storage and binary vectors for indexes. Cited on Hacker News in response to the November-2025 case-against-pgvector essay as the canonical "yes, it does scale if you quantise" rebuttal.
  • Supabase (2025 internal benchmark): pgvector with HNSW outperforms Qdrant on equivalent compute at 99% accuracy. Caveat: Supabase ships pgvector — not impartial.
  • AWS Aurora (2025): pgvector 0.7.0 + Aurora optimised reads achieves 67× faster index loading than pgvector 0.5.1.
  • ANN-benchmarks 2025: Qdrant tops the QPS chart at ~1,840 QPS on 1M-vector workloads; pgvector trails by single-digit factors but wins on JOIN-heavy workloads.

B. Embedding model choice

B.1 The MTEB shortlist

ModelDimParamsMTEB avgMax ctxLicenceDisk
nomic-embed-text-v1.5768 (Matryoshka 64–768)137M62.288,192 tokensApache 2.0274 MB
bge-base-en-v1.5768109M63.55512 tokensMIT~440 MB
bge-large-en-v1.51024335M~64.2512 tokensMIT~1.3 GB
all-MiniLM-L6-v238422.7M~56.3256 word-piecesApache 2.0~90 MB

All four are commercial-use-OK with no subscription. All four run on Ollama or sentence-transformers.

B.2 Recommendation: nomic-embed-text-v1.5 as the default

R010 lists nomic-embed-text, BGE, and MiniLM as candidates without picking. Pick nomic-embed-text-v1.5. Rationale:

  1. 8K context (vs BGE's 512) — many Vector PM artefacts (acceptance criteria, retro notes, defect repros) routinely exceed 512 tokens. BGE-base would silently truncate; nomic embeds the whole thing.
  2. Matryoshka representation learning — same model, same weights, but you can truncate the vector to 256 or 512 dims and recover ~98% of MTEB performance (61.04 at 256-dim vs 62.28 at 768-dim). This means you can ship at 768-dim and quietly reduce to 256-dim later if storage hurts, without re-running embeddings. Vector PM benefits because the storage budget per tenant is unknown until usage shows up.
  3. Apache 2.0 — clean for resale (template marketplace, PoS+ packs).
  4. Trained for retrieval specifically — has dedicated search_query: and search_document: prefixes that distinguish queries from documents. This is a measurable lift over symmetric models on RAG-style queries.
  5. Comparable MTEB to BGE-base — within the noise floor (62.28 vs 63.55) but with 16× more context.

When to choose differently:

  • Choose bge-base-en-v1.5 if context is reliably under 512 tokens AND retrieval-only quality matters more than long-doc support — BGE wins by ~1.3 MTEB points there.
  • Choose all-MiniLM-L6-v2 only as a CPU-tight fallback (22.7M params, runs comfortably on a 1-vCPU VPS at ~5 ms/embedding). Use only for prototyping or tiers where embedding cost dominates.
  • Avoid bge-large-en-v1.5 in Phase 1 — 1024-dim costs 33% more storage and ~3× more inference time for ~2 MTEB-point improvement. Wait until tier-3 quality is justified by user feedback.

B.3 Ollama as the runtime

Ollama (ollama.com) is the recommended runtime. Pros: single Docker container; HTTP API on port 11434; ollama pull nomic-embed-text downloads the model and exposes POST /api/embeddings; built-in queueing and concurrency controls; no Python dependency footprint. Model size on disk: 274 MB for nomic-embed-text. RAM footprint: ~500 MB resident with the model loaded.

Alternative: a Python sentence-transformers microservice (SentenceTransformer("nomic-ai/nomic-embed-text-v1.5") + FastAPI). More code, more deps, but lets you batch-embed in larger chunks. For Vector PM, Ollama is simpler and the throughput is enough — async worker pulls one row at a time, embeds in <100 ms on CPU.

Note: as of the Ollama page fetched, the licence isn't called out on the model card itself (Ollama's UI is sparse on metadata) — but the underlying nomic-ai weights on Hugging Face are explicitly Apache 2.0. Confirmed.

B.4 CPU vs GPU inference

nomic-embed-text on CPU: ~50–100 ms per single embedding on a modern x86 core; throughput ~10–20 embeddings/s with batching. On a single GPU (T4/A10): >1,000 embeddings/s. Vector PM doesn't need GPU — even at peak write rates (1 artefact/sec across a tenant), a single CPU worker keeps up with two orders of magnitude headroom.

C. Hybrid search patterns (lexical + semantic)

C.1 Why hybrid

The literature consistently reports the same finding: pure vector search on text wins on synonyms and meaning, pure FTS wins on rare tokens (CVE numbers, error codes, internal IDs, person names), and hybrid wins on everything else. The dbi-services and ParadeDB benchmarks both show hybrid pushing retrieval precision from ~62% (vector-only) to ~84% (RRF hybrid). For Vector PM — which mixes prose AC with literal IDs (US-00001234, CVE-2024-1234, error-code lookups) — pure vector would lose on the literal IDs every time. Hybrid is mandatory, not optional.

C.2 Reciprocal Rank Fusion in Postgres SQL

The Supabase reference function (slightly adapted for Vector PM):

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION hybrid_search(
  query_text       TEXT,
  query_embedding  vector(768),
  match_count      INT DEFAULT 10,
  full_text_weight FLOAT DEFAULT 1.0,
  semantic_weight  FLOAT DEFAULT 1.0,
  rrf_k            INT DEFAULT 50,
  user_max_visibility SMALLINT DEFAULT 0,
  sub_id           UUID
)
RETURNS SETOF artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values
LANGUAGE sql STABLE PARALLEL SAFE AS $$
WITH lexical AS (
  SELECT id,
         row_number() OVER (
           ORDER BY ts_rank_cd(search_index, websearch_to_tsquery(query_text)) DESC
         ) AS rank_ix
  FROM artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values
  WHERE search_index @@ websearch_to_tsquery(query_text)
    AND visibility <= user_max_visibility
    AND subscription_id = sub_id
  ORDER BY rank_ix
  LIMIT LEAST(match_count, 30) * 2
),
semantic AS (
  SELECT id,
         row_number() OVER (
           ORDER BY content_embedding <=> query_embedding
         ) AS rank_ix
  FROM artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values
  WHERE visibility <= user_max_visibility
    AND subscription_id = sub_id
  ORDER BY rank_ix
  LIMIT LEAST(match_count, 30) * 2
)
SELECT v.*
FROM artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values v
LEFT JOIN lexical l ON l.id = v.id
LEFT JOIN semantic s ON s.id = v.id
WHERE l.id IS NOT NULL OR s.id IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY
  COALESCE(1.0 / (rrf_k + l.rank_ix), 0.0) * full_text_weight +
  COALESCE(1.0 / (rrf_k + s.rank_ix), 0.0) * semantic_weight
  DESC
LIMIT LEAST(match_count, 30);
$$;

Two notes vs R010 section 14.6:

  1. R010's example uses raw weighted score fusion. This breaks when ts_rank and (1-cosine) live on different scales. RRF normalises by rank position, not score, which is robust to scale skew. Use the RRF form above.
  2. The visibility predicate (visibility <= user_max_visibility AND subscription_id = sub_id) goes inside each CTE, not outside. Pre-filtering — see G.4 below — is the difference between 50 ms and 5 s queries.

C.3 Re-ranking strategies

Two further options on top of RRF, both optional:

  • Cross-encoder reranking on top-50 — pgvector docs reference an example. After hybrid retrieval, run the top-50 results through a cross-encoder model (e.g. BAAI/bge-reranker-base, MIT, 280 MB) which scores query+document pairs end-to-end. Adds ~20–50 ms but lifts precision noticeably on ambiguous queries. Worth it for the dedup and template-recommendation use cases.
  • Maximum Marginal Relevance (MMR) — re-rank to favour diversity over pure similarity. Useful when results are over-clustered (e.g. "all 5 hits are slight variants of the same AC"). Implement in application code over the top-50.

D. Async indexing patterns

D.1 NOTIFY/LISTEN as written in R010 has gaps

The R010 section 14.5 trigger-fires-NOTIFY-worker-LISTENs pattern works most of the time but has three documented failure modes:

  1. At-most-once delivery. If the worker connection drops mid-message, the notification is lost. Postgres does not retry. The row's embedding is never computed and the trigger has already fired (won't fire again on read).
  2. Queue overflow on slow consumer. The Postgres NOTIFY queue is 8 GB by default, but it cannot be drained while any LISTEN session is in a long-running transaction. If a backup tool, a reporting query, or an idle psql session opens a transaction and forgets to close it, NOTIFY queue fills, and writes start failing at COMMIT. This has hit Hasura, Symfony, and Discourse in production.
  3. Worker restart loses backlog. Notifications between worker restart and LISTEN re-issue are dropped on the floor.

For Vector PM specifically: a missed embedding means a story is searchable lexically but not semantically. The user sees inconsistent search behaviour and no error. This is the worst kind of bug — silent and slow-to-discover.

D.2 Recommended pattern: outbox + NOTIFY wake-up + polling fallback

Standard durable-queue pattern, well-documented across Procrastinate, River, neoq, asynq-on-postgres:

-- 1. Outbox table — survives restarts, transactions, dropped connections
CREATE TABLE search_index_queue (
  id              BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  table_name      TEXT NOT NULL,
  row_id          UUID NOT NULL,
  enqueued_at     TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  attempted_at    TIMESTAMPTZ,
  attempts        INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  last_error      TEXT,
  UNIQUE (table_name, row_id)  -- coalesce repeated updates
);

CREATE INDEX ON search_index_queue (enqueued_at) WHERE attempted_at IS NULL;

-- 2. Trigger writes to outbox, then signals
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION queue_search_index_update() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO search_index_queue (table_name, row_id)
  VALUES (TG_TABLE_NAME, NEW.id)
  ON CONFLICT (table_name, row_id) DO UPDATE
    SET enqueued_at = now(), attempted_at = NULL;
  PERFORM pg_notify('search_index_wakeup', '');
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- 3. Worker LISTENs, but ALSO polls every N seconds for safety
-- Worker pseudocode:
--   loop:
--     rows := SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED LIMIT 32 FROM search_index_queue
--               WHERE attempted_at IS NULL OR attempted_at < now() - interval '5 minutes'
--     if rows: embed + UPDATE field_values + DELETE from search_index_queue
--     else: wait for NOTIFY OR 30-second timeout

Properties:

  • At-least-once delivery — outbox row is durable; failed attempts retry.
  • Coalesces hot rows — UNIQUE constraint means rapid edits collapse to one work item.
  • Self-healing on worker restart — backlog is read from the outbox, not from in-memory NOTIFY state.
  • NOTIFY is a wake-up hint, not the queue — losing it just means waiting up to 30 s for the next poll, not losing data.
  • SKIP LOCKED means many workers can run in parallel without coordination.

Cost: one extra table, ~30 lines of trigger code, and a 30-second floor on indexing latency in pathological scenarios. Worth it.

D.3 Alternatives evaluated

OptionVerdict
pg_cron running embed function on a scheduleWorks but couples embedding to a schedule; no real-time signal. Reasonable for nightly bulk re-embed; bad for live indexing.
Debezium / logical replicationOverkill — adds a Kafka-shaped operational surface for what a Postgres outbox already does.
River queue (Go, Postgres-native)Excellent if Vector PM standardises on a real job queue. Same outbox pattern, plus retries, scheduling, dashboards. Worth adopting if other async work appears.
asynq, neoqSame family as River. Pick by language fit (River = Go, Procrastinate = Python).

D.4 Backfills and re-embedding

Two scenarios need a plan:

  1. Adding embedding to an existing table. One-shot INSERT INTO search_index_queue (table_name, row_id) SELECT 'foo', id FROM foo; dumps every row into the outbox; the worker drains it. The Sequin/Salesforce production write-up reports ~50M rows/hour through pgvector + parallel workers on a 256 GB RDS, full backfill in <2 days.
  2. Embedding model change (different dimension). Multi-step migration:
    • Add second column content_embedding_v2 vector(N').
    • Backfill it via the queue.
    • Switch read path to v2 once 100% populated.
    • Drop v1 column.
    This is the documented "shadow deployment" / "zero-downtime migration" pattern — both Google and several pgvector users describe it. Plan for one such migration when nomic-embed-text-v2 ships, and at least one more if Vector PM later moves to a 1024-dim BGE-large.

E. The 15 product use cases — pressure tested

E.1 Search-and-compose authoring

Pattern: user types a query in the field-search bar; hybrid search returns top-N matching _field_values rows across all artefacts the user can see; user clicks to copy.
Why pgvector fits: visibility filter and subscription filter are ordinary WHERE clauses; semantic match finds re-worded AC; lexical match finds literal CVE/ID strings.
Failure modes: if pre-filtering isn't done correctly (HNSW filter applied post-index instead of inside the CTE), latency spikes 50× on tenants with millions of rows. Mitigation: always pre-filter by tenant; consider a per-tenant partial index for very large tenants.

E.2 Duplicate detection on artefact create

Pattern: on save, embed the new artefact title+content, query top-5 by cosine; if any score > 0.85 (cosine similarity), warn the user.
Why pgvector fits: threshold queries are trivial in SQL; visibility filter scopes to relevant universe.
Failure modes: threshold tuning is dataset-specific. Literature says 0.9+ for product titles; 0.85 is a reasonable default for prose AC; retros need ~0.8. Plan to make this configurable per artefact type.

E.3 Smart linking suggestions

Pattern: nightly job (or on-demand) embeds each artefact, finds top-N nearest neighbours of a different type (defect → user-stories), surfaces unlinked candidates.
Why pgvector fits: cross-table queries via UNION ALL across artefacts_* tables; type filter is a column predicate.
Failure modes: noisy results when artefacts are short/generic. Mitigation: require minimum content length before suggesting; require min similarity 0.7+; let users dismiss suggestions persistently.

E.4 Auto-tagging from content

Pattern: embed artefact, find K nearest tagged neighbours, propose union of their tags weighted by similarity (kNN classification).
Why pgvector fits: standard kNN over the existing index.
Failure modes: tags drift over time and the kNN approach freezes the past's vocabulary. Mitigation: weight by recency; periodically re-evaluate.

E.5 Template recommendation

Pattern: compute centroid embedding per template_form_id; on draft, embed and find nearest centroid.
Why pgvector fits: centroids are just rows; pgvector handles them identically to any other vector.
Failure modes: centroids become stale as templates evolve; refresh on a schedule. With small templates (<10 instances) the centroid is meaningless — fall back to nearest-individual-artefact match below 10 instances.

E.6 Risk pattern matching

Pattern: embed new story; find historical artefacts that became blocked, missed sprint, or hit incident; surface as "3 stories like this had problems — see them."
Why pgvector fits: outcome is a column predicate (blocked = TRUE) joined to vector match.
Failure modes: survivorship bias — only blocked stories surface; non-blocked similar stories disappear. Show both numerator and denominator ("3 of 47 similar stories were blocked").

E.7 Retro insight clustering

Pattern: embed each sticky note; cluster via k-means or HDBSCAN in application layer; group by cluster.
Why pgvector fits: pgvector is a similarity store, not a clustering library — but exporting embeddings to a Python clustering pass is trivial.
Failure modes: small retros (<30 notes) don't cluster meaningfully; threshold to skip clustering when N is small.

E.8 Cross-team knowledge surfacing

Pattern: identical to E.1 but with visibility > 0 (workspace) — the user types and sees other teams' content.
Why pgvector fits: visibility filter is the only difference.
Failure modes: privacy. Cell-level visibility must be enforced by RLS, not just by application predicate. See G.5.

E.9 AI agent grounding (RAG)

Pattern: AI agent receives a user question; agent embeds the question; queries pgvector for top-K relevant artefacts; passes results to LLM as context.
Why pgvector fits: textbook RAG. The visibility filter is the killer feature — RAG over Vector PM data is automatically scoped to what the calling user is allowed to see.
Failure modes: retrieval quality dominates LLM answer quality. Hybrid > pure vector. Top-K should be 10–20, not 3 — modern context windows are large enough.

E.10 Dashboard auto-summaries

Pattern: for a time window, embed all writes, cluster, generate one paragraph per cluster via LLM.
Why pgvector fits: retrieval portion is fine.
Failure modes: summarisation quality is LLM-bound, not vector-bound; LLM cost scales with cluster count. Cap clusters to 5–7.

E.11 Onboarding context

Pattern: for a new joiner, retrieve top-K artefacts most central to their team's current sprint (nearest neighbours to sprint centroid).
Why pgvector fits: standard kNN.
Failure modes: cold start — a brand-new sprint has no centroid. Fall back to most-recently-touched.

E.12 Page-builder block recommendations

Pattern: embed the user's current page config_json (text-bearing parts only); find similar pages; recommend their additional blocks.
Why pgvector fits: reuses the same index.
Failure modes: collaborative-filtering signals are weak with small N (early product). Defer to Phase 2.

E.13 Template marketplace match-making

Pattern: embed each template's description + sample artefacts; embed the buyer's existing artefacts; suggest templates with high cosine to buyer's centroid.
Why pgvector fits: simple kNN.
Failure modes: needs a marketplace first. Phase 3+ concern.

E.14 Field-value reuse mining

Pattern: periodically cluster _field_values by similarity; surface large clusters to padmins as candidates for promotion to a reusable snippet.
Why pgvector fits: embeddings already exist.
Failure modes: noise — humans must confirm each cluster. Treat as a recommendation queue, not an automation.

E.15 Comment thread routing

Pattern: on a new comment, find unresolved comments elsewhere with similar topic; surface in side panel.
Why pgvector fits: page_block_comments gets an embedding column; same query.
Failure modes: false-positive routing erodes trust faster than missed routing. Conservative threshold (~0.85+).

F. Documentation use case (10 sub-cases)

F.1 Semantic doc search

Pattern: hybrid search over doc_pages.content_plain_text + content_embedding.
Why pgvector fits: identical to E.1 but on the docs schema.
Failure modes: keyword queries ("clone portfolio item") want lexical; conceptual queries ("copy a thing") want semantic. Hybrid covers both.

F.2 Method/SDK discovery

Pattern: embed each method's description + signature; user query embeds against this index.
Why pgvector fits: doc_code_examples.code_embedding already in R010 14.9.1.
Failure modes: method names are short — embedding alone is weak. Embed the full description plus signature, not just name.

F.3 RAG over docs

Pattern: chat assistant retrieves top-K doc chunks for the user's question, generates answer with citations.
Why pgvector fits: standard.
Failure modes: hallucinated method names. Mitigation: post-process LLM output to verify cited methods exist in doc_pages.

F.4 Cross-reference docs ↔ code examples

Pattern: for each artefact mentioning a method name, suggest the doc page; for each doc page, suggest example artefacts.
Why pgvector fits: two cross-table cosine queries.
Failure modes: requires consistent method naming in artefacts (rare). Consider regex extraction first, vector second.

F.5 Code-comment to doc-page suggestion

Pattern: at PR time, embed the JSDoc/Go-doc; flag if very similar to an existing doc — possible API surface drift.
Why pgvector fits: CI tool calls a similarity endpoint.
Failure modes: developer fatigue if too noisy. Threshold conservatively (>0.92).

F.6 Versioned doc similarity

Pattern: compare embeddings of v1 page vs v2 page; surface largest deltas as auto-changelog seeds.
Why pgvector fits: stored versioned vectors.
Failure modes: embedding diffs are noisy on small edits. Combine with text-diff size.

F.7 Support deflection

Pattern: on form submit, search docs + prior tickets; show top-3 before letting submission through.
Why pgvector fits: standard hybrid query.
Failure modes: the often-cited "Stripe-style 40% deflection" number doesn't have a primary source — couldn't find it in Stripe's published material despite searching. Treat it as plausible-but-unverified marketing-speak; expect 10–25% deflection on launch and tune up.

F.8 Tutorial path generation

Pattern: embed user's goal; find ordered sequence of doc pages by combining similarity with prerequisites.
Why pgvector fits: retrieval is one part; ordering needs an LLM or graph.
Failure modes: doc dependency graph isn't structural data — must be authored. Start with similarity-only ordering.

F.9 Multilingual docs

Pattern: embed-then-translate (multilingual model embeds source-language docs, queries can be in any language) is preferred over translate-then-embed.
Why pgvector fits: agnostic.
Failure modes: nomic-embed-text-v1.5 is English-mostly. For multilingual, pivot to nomic-embed-text-v1.5's multilingual sibling or BAAI/bge-m3. Plan to allow per-tenant model choice if multilingual becomes a real customer ask.

F.10 SDK-aware chat for AI agents

Pattern: agent calls a Vector PM endpoint that returns docs + signatures relevant to the agent's stated intent.
Why pgvector fits: just an authenticated retrieval API.
Failure modes: rate limiting, abuse. Standard API protections apply.

G. Risks, gotchas, and what we should NOT do

G.1 Where pgvector falls down

  • Memory-pinned HNSW. pgvector keeps the entire HNSW graph in shared_buffers / OS cache for low-latency queries. Qdrant memory-maps from disk; you can serve more vectors per GB of RAM. At >50M vectors per tenant this becomes the dominant cost.
  • Single-process bottleneck. Postgres is single-master. Heavy embedding workloads contend with OLTP traffic on the same instance. Mitigation: read replica for vector queries when needed.
  • No native filtered ANN. When you query with a filter (visibility, tenant, archived), Postgres can pre-filter (use the b-tree on subscription_id, then scan vectors) or post-filter (use HNSW first, then drop non-matching rows). Pre-filter wins on selective filters; post-filter wins on broad ones. Postgres' planner gets this right most of the time but can pick wrong on edge cases — the case-against-pgvector essay calls this out as the source of "50ms vs 5s" surprise. Mitigation: EXPLAIN ANALYZE the production queries and add covering indexes where the planner needs help.

G.2 When we'd actually need to migrate off pgvector

  • Tenant vector counts > 50M with frequent updates — HNSW maintenance becomes hot.
  • Filtered queries with very tight selectivity (single user, single product) where pgvector planner picks the wrong filter order. Tenant partitioning usually fixes this before migration is justified.
  • Multi-vector documents (image + text + structured) needing hybrid re-ranking inside the index — Weaviate's strength. Vector PM doesn't have this need.
  • QPS > 5,000/sec sustained — Qdrant or Vespa win at this rate. Vector PM, even at scale, will be 1–2 orders of magnitude below.

Verdict: migration triggers are 2027+ Phase-4 concerns. Architect with the escape valve in mind (R010 14.3 already does — "swap to Qdrant is a backend change, not a schema change") but don't pay for it now.

G.3 Embedding drift and model versioning

The biggest operational hazard. Three rules:

  1. Pin the model version (nomic-embed-text-v1.5, not nomic-embed-text:latest) in the worker config. Same input, same vector, forever.
  2. Store the model version with each embedding row — add an embedding_model TEXT column. When mixing two model versions during migration, you can route queries correctly.
  3. Plan for one full re-embed every ~18–24 months. Better embedding models will exist; you'll want them. Budget the operational cost into the roadmap.

G.4 Index bloat and pre-filtering

HNSW grows as ~2-3× data size. Soft-deleted rows stay in the index until VACUUM. For a Vector PM tenant with 1M artefacts at 768 dims (~3 KB raw vector + index overhead): expect ~10 GB of index. Across 100 tenants that's 1 TB — already non-trivial. Mitigations:

  • Partial index (WHERE archived_at IS NULL) — already in R010 14.2. Keep.
  • Per-tenant partitioning of the largest _field_values tables once any one tenant exceeds ~500k rows.
  • Aggressive autovacuum schedule on tables with high update churn.
  • Switch to halfvec (fp16) once any one tenant's vectors exceed RAM. Recall loss is <1% on retrieval tasks.

G.5 Privacy and tenant isolation

Embeddings can leak content. Two known attack vectors: (1) given a vector, recover an approximation of the source text via gradient inversion; (2) given vector-search access to another tenant's index, fingerprint their content. Mitigations for Vector PM:

  • Postgres Row-Level Security (RLS) on every embedding table. Application predicates can be forgotten; RLS is enforced by the database. The pattern is: ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON ... USING (subscription_id = current_setting('app.current_subscription_id')::uuid); This is the AWS Aurora multi-tenant RAG pattern.
  • Set app.current_subscription_id at the start of every request — the application's tenant-resolution middleware. Never let untrusted clients set app.* variables.
  • Visibility filter inside RLS too for the cell-level case. R010 4.1's WHERE clause should be moved into a CREATE POLICY, not enforced in application code only.
  • Per-tenant or per-product partial indexes when a tenant grows large enough that cross-tenant index sharing becomes a perceptible concern.

G.6 Cost of embedding at scale

Self-hosted Ollama is free for compute, paid only in VPS time. Per-row cost on CPU: ~50–100 ms compute. At 1 row/sec sustained, that's a single CPU core busy 5–10% of the time — trivial. At 100 rows/sec (very heavy multi-tenant write rate), one CPU is saturated; scale horizontally by running 4–8 worker processes against the same outbox. For Vector PM's projected scale, embedding cost is a rounding error compared to Postgres infrastructure cost. The crossover where SaaS embeddings (OpenAI, Voyage, Cohere) becomes cheaper than self-hosted is somewhere above 10M embeddings/day per workload — not in the foreseeable Vector PM range.

H. Recommendations summary (changes to R010)

  1. R010 14.2 — change USING ivfflat to USING hnsw (content_embedding vector_cosine_ops). Better recall, no centroid drift, no load-first constraint.
  2. R010 14.4 — pick nomic-embed-text-v1.5 as the named default. 768-dim, Apache 2.0, 8K context, Matryoshka-truncatable.
  3. R010 14.5 — replace bare NOTIFY/LISTEN with outbox + NOTIFY wake-up + polling. 30 lines of trigger + 1 table; eliminates silent embedding loss.
  4. R010 14.6 — switch the example query from weighted score fusion to RRF. Use the Supabase reference function adapted with visibility/tenant predicates inside each CTE.
  5. Add to R010 — embedding_model column on every embedding-bearing table. Future-proof for model migrations.
  6. Add to R010 — Postgres RLS policies on every tenant-scoped table, with app.current_subscription_id session variable. Defence-in-depth over application-layer filters.
  7. Add to R010 — partial index (WHERE archived_at IS NULL) on every HNSW index. Already implied; make it a hard rule.
  8. Add to R010 — phase plan for halfvec migration. Default to vector(768) in Phase 1; switch to halfvec(768) any tenant once its hot vectors exceed shared_buffers. Same recall, half the RAM.

Gaps

  • Stripe's specific support-deflection number from RAG-over-docs (R010 cites ~40%). Could not find a primary Stripe source; the figure may be folklore. Recommend treating as plausible but unverified.
  • Did not benchmark nomic-embed-text-v1.5 head-to-head against bge-base-en-v1.5 on Vector PM-shaped data (acceptance criteria, retro notes, defect repros). Recommend a one-sprint spike where ~500 representative pairs are scored by both models and quality is measured via human-judged precision@10.
  • Could not retrieve the live MTEB leaderboard table directly — leaderboard scores cited come from individual model cards (Hugging Face) where the model author publishes their own MTEB run. These can be optimistic by 0.5–1.5 points vs the central leaderboard.
  • Production cost data for Ollama vs sentence-transformers Python service under sustained load: didn't find independent benchmarks. Both should be stress-tested before Phase 1 production launch.
  • The case-against-pgvector article (Simon Willison, Nov 2025) couldn't be fully extracted (paywall/JS). Summary above is from the HN comment digest, which is consistent with the IT industry's current consensus but should be re-read directly when accessible.
